title: Expired Fire Extinguisher Tag
scene_description: You notice a fire extinguisher mounted on the wall in the server room. The inspection tag shows it was last checked 14 months ago. Dust has gathered on the handle.
explanation: This is a non-compliance under Fire Safety & Emergency Preparedness. Under the Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005, all fire-fighting equipment must be maintained in good working order. An inspection overdue by 14 months means there is no assurance this extinguisher will function in an emergency.

title: Properly Marked Emergency Exit
scene_description: You walk down a corridor and spot a clearly illuminated green 'Emergency Exit' sign above a door. The door opens easily and leads to a clear, unobstructed stairwell. The sign is clean and well-lit.
explanation: This is actually compliant. The emergency exit is properly signed with an illuminated sign, the door opens freely, and the escape route is unobstructed. This meets the requirements of the Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005 and BS 5499 for emergency signage.
source_reference: UK Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005, BS 5499

title: Unlabeled Electrical Panel
scene_description: In a hallway near the server closet, you find an electrical distribution panel. The panel door is closed but there are no hazard warning labels, no voltage markings, and no 'Danger: Electricity' signage anywhere on or near it.
explanation: This is a non-compliance under Electrical Safety. The Electricity at Work Regulations 1989 require that electrical systems are properly maintained and that hazards are clearly identified. Additionally, the Health and Safety (Safety Signs and Signals) Regulations 1996 require appropriate warning signs where electrical hazards exist.

title: Blocked Fire Exit Corridor
scene_description: The corridor leading to the fire exit on the ground floor has several large cardboard boxes stacked along one side, narrowing the path to about half its normal width. A sign on the wall reads 'Fire Exit — Keep Clear'.
explanation: This is a non-compliance under Fire Safety & Emergency Preparedness. The Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005 requires that escape routes must be kept clear and free from obstruction at all times. Partially blocking a fire exit corridor with boxes creates a serious hazard during an evacuation.
source_reference: UK Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005, Article 14

title: Properly Set Up Workstation
scene_description: You inspect a desk in the open-plan office. The monitor is at eye level on an adjustable arm, the chair has lumbar support and is adjusted to the correct height, and a footrest is tucked underneath. The employee has a separate keyboard and mouse.
explanation: This is actually compliant. The workstation meets the requirements of the Health and Safety (Display Screen Equipment) Regulations 1992. The adjustable monitor, proper chair, footrest, and separate keyboard/mouse all demonstrate a properly assessed and set up DSE workstation.
source_reference: UK Health and Safety (Display Screen Equipment) Regulations 1992

title: Overloaded Extension Lead
scene_description: Under a desk near the server closet, you spot a single extension lead with a 4-gang socket. All four sockets are occupied, and another extension lead is plugged into one of them, powering three more devices. The cables are warm to the touch.
explanation: This is a non-compliance under Electrical Safety. Daisy-chaining extension leads is a well-known fire and electrocution risk. The Electricity at Work Regulations 1989 require that electrical systems are used in a manner that prevents danger. Overloading circuits with daisy-chained extensions can cause overheating and fire.
source_reference: UK Electricity at Work Regulations 1989, Regulation 4

title: Suspicious Stain Near Server Room
scene_description: You notice a dark stain on the carpet tiles just outside the server closet. It could be a water leak from the ceiling, a coffee spill, or something from the cleaning crew. There's no wet floor sign, but the stain appears dry.
explanation: This is ambiguous — more information is needed. The stain could be harmless (old coffee spill) or could indicate an ongoing water leak that poses slip hazards or damage to electrical equipment in the server room. A good auditor would flag this for further investigation rather than making assumptions.
source_reference: UK Workplace (Health, Safety and Welfare) Regulations 1992

title: Missing Risk Assessment Folder
scene_description: In the manager's office, you ask to see the fire risk assessment documentation. The manager searches through filing cabinets and desk drawers but cannot locate the folder. 'I'm sure it's here somewhere,' they say. 'We definitely had one done last year.'
explanation: This is a non-compliance under Permits, Signage & Documentation. Under the Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999, employers with 5 or more employees must record their risk assessments. Being unable to produce documentation on request suggests either inadequate record-keeping or that the assessment was never properly completed.
source_reference: UK Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999, Regulation 3
